name: API paradigms & realtime
description: REST, GraphQL, gRPC, SOAP/RPC and realtime transports — choosing and defending an API style. From the 'API paradigmák' prep doc.

topics.name: GraphQL
topics.description: Query language with a single endpoint, client-specified fields, one round trip.

topics.name: gRPC
topics.description: Contract-first RPC over HTTP/2 with protobuf, streaming, strong typing.

topics.name: Realtime transports
topics.description: Polling, SSE, WebSocket and webhooks for pushing data to clients.

questions.title: REST vs GraphQL
questions.prompt: How would you decide between REST and GraphQL for a new API?
questions.answerStructureHint: Anchor in client needs; over/under-fetching and one round trip vs caching, complexity and versioning.

expectedPoints.label: Frames around client needs
expectedPoints.description: Decides based on how varied and demanding the clients' data needs are.

expectedPoints.label: GraphQL solves over/under-fetching
expectedPoints.description: Client picks exactly the fields it needs in one request; avoids many round trips.

expectedPoints.label: REST strengths and GraphQL costs
expectedPoints.description: REST is simple, cache-friendly (HTTP caching); GraphQL adds server complexity, caching and rate-limiting challenges.

followUps.prompt: GraphQL can trigger an N+1 problem on the server. What is it and how would you address it?

questions.title: When to use gRPC
questions.prompt: When would you choose gRPC over a REST or GraphQL API?
questions.answerStructureHint: Internal service-to-service, performance, streaming and contracts vs browser support and tooling.

expectedPoints.label: Service-to-service performance
expectedPoints.description: gRPC suits internal microservice calls: binary protobuf over HTTP/2, low latency, streaming.

expectedPoints.label: Contract-first with generated code
expectedPoints.description: Proto files define a strict contract and generate typed client/server stubs.

expectedPoints.label: Knows the browser/tooling limitation
expectedPoints.description: Not natively callable from browsers (needs grpc-web/proxy); harder to debug than JSON.

followUps.prompt: gRPC supports streaming. Give an example where server or bidirectional streaming is the right fit.

questions.title: Realtime: polling vs SSE vs WebSocket vs webhook
questions.prompt: Compare polling, server-sent events, WebSocket and webhooks. When does each fit?
questions.answerStructureHint: Direction, who initiates, connection cost, and the classic use cases for each.

expectedPoints.label: Distinguishes by direction and initiator
expectedPoints.description: Polling/SSE are client-pull/server-push one-way; WebSocket is bidirectional; webhooks are server-to-server callbacks.

expectedPoints.label: Right use case for each
expectedPoints.description: Polling for simple/low-frequency, SSE for notifications/feeds, WebSocket for chat/live collaboration, webhook for third-party events.

expectedPoints.label: Mentions connection cost / scaling
expectedPoints.description: Persistent connections (WebSocket) cost server resources and complicate load balancing.

followUps.prompt: A webhook delivery can fail. How do you make webhook consumption reliable?
